title: Teams and Roles
description: Configure context teams, shared teams, and team templates. Understand role types, team instances, and how role resolution drives access control and workflow assignment.

topics.title: Team Management Fundamentals

content.text: Context managers such as product and project managers use simplified team management to indirectly manage access control and workflow assignment. Rather than configuring security policies directly, they assign participants to roles on a team page, and Windchill resolves those assignments into access control rules and workflow task routing.
The Context Manager's Role

Context managers work with teams by adding participants to predefined roles. Each role carries meaning in terms of what data a participant can access and which workflow tasks they receive. The manager does not need to understand the underlying access control policies or workflow routing logic — assigning the right people to the right roles is sufficient.

Context managers do not need full administrative knowledge of Windchill security. The team page abstracts the complexity of access control and workflow configuration into a simple participant-to-role assignment model.
How Teams Drive Access and Workflows

rows: Participants in roles determine what data they can access
Generic roles map to specific users who receive workflow tasks
Teams map generic business roles to specific participants

content.prompt: Match each team management concept to its description.

keyTakeaways: Context managers assign participants to roles, indirectly controlling access and workflow routing.
Generic roles define which users receive workflow tasks and what data they can access.
Teams bridge the gap between business role definitions and Windchill security enforcement.

topics.title: Context Team Architecture and Roles

content.text: Every product, project, library, and program in Windchill has exactly one context team. This team manages context roles visible on the Members table and context groups generated in the database. Understanding how roles, groups, and the special Team Members group interact is essential for configuring access control correctly.
Context Roles and Context Groups

The Team Members group is a superset of all non-Guest participants. Editing out-of-the-box access control rules on this group affects every team member. Always review rules carefully before modifying this group.
Resource Bundle Roles vs Context Roles

content.prompt: Arrange the steps in the correct order for what happens when a user is added to a context role.
items: User is added to the context role
System generates a corresponding context group entry
User is added to the Team Members group
Access control rules for the context group take effect

keyTakeaways: Every context has one context team with roles visible on the Members table and corresponding groups in Policy Administration.
The Team Members group collects all non-Guest participants and has no corresponding role on the team page.
Resource bundle roles can be used in workflows and lifecycles; context team roles are limited to access control resolution.
Adding a user to a context role automatically generates a context group entry and adds the user to the Team Members group.

topics.title: Shared Teams

content.text: Shared teams are defined at the organization level and can be used across multiple products, libraries, projects, and programs. They provide a centralized way to manage team membership when the same participants and roles apply to many contexts.
How Shared Teams Work

cards.back: Defined at the organization level and shared across multiple products, libraries, projects, or programs.
When enabled, context managers can add roles and members locally but cannot remove shared team entries.
Updates to a shared team are reflected in all associated contexts. Use Show Team Uses to see affected contexts.

content.text: Always use the Show Team Uses query before making changes to a shared team. This report lists every context that uses the shared team, helping you understand the full impact of your updates.

content.prompt: Match each team type to its defining characteristic.

pairs.left: Defined at product or project level
Can be used across multiple contexts

keyTakeaways: Shared teams are defined at the organization level and reused across multiple contexts.
Context managers can extend shared teams locally when the option is enabled but cannot remove shared entries.
Changes to a shared team automatically propagate to all associated contexts.

topics.title: Team Templates and Team Instances

content.text: Team templates define the roles needed for advanced business processes such as promotion requests and change workflows. When an object initiates a process, Windchill generates a team instance by resolving roles and participants from multiple sources in a specific order.
Team Template Definition

Team templates can be created at the site, organization, or context level. When defining a template, you specify its name, description, and enabled status, then assign roles drawn from the resource bundle. These roles define the positions that must be filled when a process starts.
Team Instance Resolution

content.prompt: Arrange the team instance resolution steps in the correct order.
items: Team Template adds roles to the team instance
Life Cycle Template adds any missing roles
Context Team populates participants into existing roles
Workflow adds any missing process roles

content.text: Once a team instance is created, it is unaffected by subsequent changes to the team template or context team. Only team instances created after a change will reflect the updated configuration.
Best Practices

rows: Use context team as master for role resolution
Simplifies administration — one place to manage participants
Don't add all roles to templates as a safety net
Creates confusion with long lists of unused and unresolved roles
Only resolve org-level roles in team templates
Product and project managers should not manage organization-level positions
Same role name must resolve to the same person across all workflows

keyTakeaways: Team templates define roles for business processes and can be created at site, organization, or context level.
Team instance resolution follows a specific order: team template, lifecycle template, context team, then workflow.
Once created, team instances are unaffected by changes to their source templates or context teams.
Use the context team as the master source for participant resolution to simplify administration.

topics.title: Exercise - Administer Roles and Context Teams

content.text: In this exercise you will add a custom role to an organization and then configure a product context team by adding roles, removing unnecessary roles, and assigning participants.
Sign in as bpatterson/ptc to complete this exercise.

content.title: Administer Roles and Context Teams
content.objective: Add a custom Editor role to the PTC Publications organization and configure the product context team with appropriate roles and members.

steps.detail: Browse to the PTC Publications organization and select the Roles link.
Click the New roles to organization icon, type Editor, and click OK.
steps.hint: Verify the new role appears in the Roles table with Visible status.

steps.detail: Navigate to Recent Products, expand the PTC New Publication Template product, and click the Team link.

steps.action: Add the Editor role to the context team
steps.detail: Select Actions > Add Roles, select Editor from the role list, and click OK.
Select Change Admin I, II, III, Change Review Board, Option Manager, Package Creator, and Variance Approver, then click the Remove roles or participants icon.
steps.hint: Removing unnecessary roles is best practice for product templates.

steps.action: Add a participant to the Editor role
steps.detail: Click the Add participants icon in the Editor row, search for David Griffin (dgriffin), and add him.

keyTakeaways: Custom roles are added at the organization level and then become available to context teams.
Context teams should only include roles relevant to the product or project to reduce clutter.
Removing unnecessary roles from product templates is a best practice for streamlined team management.

topics.title: Exercise - Use Shared Teams

content.text: In this exercise you will create a shared team at the organization level, enable local extensions, and then create a new product that uses the shared team.
Complete the previous exercise before starting this one. You must be signed in as bpatterson/ptc.

content.title: Use Shared Teams
content.objective: Create a shared team for PTC Publications and test it by creating a new product that uses the shared team.

steps.detail: Browse to Organizations > PTC Publications > Teams.

steps.action: Create the PTC Publication Manuals shared team
steps.detail: Click New shared team, type PTC Publication Manuals, and select the checkbox for Allow team to be extended locally.

steps.action: Add a member to the shared team
steps.detail: Open the shared team details and add David Griffin to the Members role.

tasks.title: Create a Product Using the Shared Team

steps.detail: Navigate to Recent Products > View All and click New Product.
Click Find to search for shared teams, select PTC Publication Manuals, and enable local extensions.
steps.hint: Select the Allow roles and/or members to be added locally checkbox.

steps.detail: Name the product Maintenance Manuals, select the General Product template, and click OK.
Browse to PTC Publications > Teams, right-click PTC Publication Manuals, and select Show Team Uses.
steps.hint: The Maintenance Manuals product should appear in the report.

keyTakeaways: Shared teams are created at the organization level and can be associated with multiple products.
The Allow team to be extended locally option lets context managers add roles and members without modifying the shared team.
Show Team Uses provides a report of all contexts associated with a shared team.

topics.title: Exercise - Create Team Templates

content.text: In this exercise you will create a team template for the Agenda document type, assign roles from the resource bundle, and configure an object initialization rule to apply the template automatically.
Complete the previous exercises before starting this one. You must be signed in as bpatterson/ptc.

content.title: Create Team Templates
content.objective: Create a team template for the Agenda document type and assign it using object initialization rules.

steps.detail: Navigate to PTC New Publication Template > Utilities and select Team Administration.
Click the New Team template icon, type Agenda Team in the Name field.
Click Add Role, search for Manag* to add Managing Editor, then search for proj* to add Project Engineer.
steps.hint: Confirm both roles appear in the table before clicking OK.

tasks.title: Assign the Template to a Document Type

steps.detail: Browse to PTC New Publication Template > Utilities and select Object Initialization Rules Administration.
Click New Object Initialization Rule, name it Agenda Rule, and set the Type to Agenda (under Document).
Click Choose File and browse to the Agenda_Default_TeamOIR.xml file.
steps.hint: This file defines the lifecycle and team template for new Agenda documents.

steps.detail: Create a new Agenda document named Agenda Team Test and verify the Team Template is set to Agenda Team.
steps.hint: Check the Information page to confirm the team template assignment.

keyTakeaways: Team templates define the roles needed for specific business processes or document types.
Object initialization rules automatically assign team templates when new objects are created.
Roles added to team templates come from the resource bundle and are used for workflow and lifecycle resolution.
